sections.label: ① Client → Gateway
decisions: Three client types: browser, mobile, 3rd-party API consumer
Edge layer (CDN + LB) sits between clients and gateway
API Gateway is the single entry point — handles routing, auth, rate limiting

sections.label: ② Service Mesh
annotations: Synchronous calls use gRPC for speed, REST for payments (3rd-party SDK constraint)
decisions: Order Service is the hub — orchestrates user, product, and payment lookups
Async layer (message queue) decouples payment confirmations and notifications
Service mesh sidecars handle discovery and circuit breaking transparently

sections.label: ③ Data Layer
annotations: Payments DB is PCI-isolated: separate network, encrypted at rest, audit logging
decisions: Database-per-service: each service owns its schema exclusively
PostgreSQL for relational data (users, orders, payments), MongoDB for flexible product catalog
Redis shared for sessions, rate limiting, and caching — acceptable shared dependency
Elasticsearch synced from Product Service for full-text search

sections.label: ④ Observability
annotations: Correlation ID assigned at gateway propagates through all services, tying signals together
decisions: Three pillars: traces (OpenTelemetry), structured logs (JSON), metrics (counters/histograms)
OpenTelemetry Collector as the unified pipeline for traces and metrics
Fluentd/Vector for log aggregation — separate pipeline because logs are high-volume
Grafana alerts → PagerDuty for on-call escalation

sections.label: ⑤ Zoom: Order Service Internals
annotations: Saga pattern: each step has a compensating action, failures run compensation in reverse order
5-min TTL on inventory hold prevents phantom stock
decisions: Saga orchestration over choreography — Order Service controls the sequence explicitly
Optimistic locking with TTL for inventory reservation (not pessimistic DB locks)
Compensation runs in reverse: refund first, then release inventory
Events emitted only after DB confirm — no premature notifications

sections.label: ⑥ Full Round Trip (Sequence)
annotations: Sequence ties together sections ①②⑤ into one timeline
Solid arrows = requests, dashed arrows = responses
Async events fire after response returns to client
decisions: Sequence diagram chosen over flowchart — shows participants and time, not just transformations
10 participants cover the full stack from client to notification
Async events shown with -) arrow — fire-and-forget after main response

sections.label: ⑦ JWT Authentication
annotations: Two sub-sections: sequence diagram (who calls whom) + flowchart (decision logic)
Redis blacklist adds revocation to stateless JWT auth
Four error exits: missing token, bad signature, expired, revoked
decisions: Sequence diagram for message flow — shows 4 participants over time with alt blocks for error paths
Flowchart for validation logic — branching decisions are best argued as a decision tree
Redis blacklist check makes JWT revocation possible without making auth stateful
Separate 401 (auth failure) vs 403 (authorization/revocation) HTTP status codes
